metric_value: Intel Xeon with EM64T triggers broad shift toward 64-bit computing; initially for applications constrained by 32-bit 4GB memory limit
notes: Intel self-authored white paper; confidence high for factual claims, medium for market predictions

metric_name: IDC Itanium server market size (base year)
metric_value: Itanium-based server market less than $1 billion in 2003
notes: IDC Server group forecast; Al Gillen and Jean Bozman IDC Analysts June 2004

metric_value: IDC forecasts Itanium-based server market to grow from less than $1 billion (2003) to more than $8 billion in 2008
notes: IDC prediction; actual outcome: Itanium market peaked ~$4B and declined post-2010

metric_value: Itanium server market did not reach $8 billion. By 2008, Itanium-based system revenue was approximately $4.4B annually (HP alone), declining to $3.5B by end-2009. x86-64 (EM64T/AMD64) dominated general workloads as predicted. Itanium was discontinued in 2021.
notes: Itanium market did not reach $8B; x86-64 (EM64T) dominated general workloads; Itanium discontinued 2021 | Itanium did not reach $8B; peaked at ~$4.4B (HP) by 2008, then declined. x86-64 dominated. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Itanium (market share section)

metric_name: Itanium architecture use case: business-critical data tier
metric_value: Itanium architecture remains platform of choice for most demanding business-critical data tier applications: high-end database and business intelligence
notes: Consistent with Intel positioning

metric_name: EM64T use case: general purpose applications
metric_value: Intel Xeon with EM64T preferable for general-purpose applications: web, mail, digital content creation, mechanical CAD, EDA; mixed 32/64-bit environments
notes: Correct segmentation; EM64T became dominant for mainstream server workloads

metric_value: Legacy mode (32-bit OS + apps), Compatibility mode (64-bit OS + 32-bit apps), 64-bit mode (full 64-bit OS + apps)
notes: Backward compatibility as key EM64T advantage over pure Itanium

metric_value: Performance boost of up to 50% for existing 32-bit applications on EM64T platforms vs prior Xeon platforms
notes: Intel internal test figure; typical range 10-40%; outliers above 50%

metric_value: IDC: New system shipments based on 32-bit x86 processors will be swept away over time by x86-64 designs
notes: Correct; x86-64 completely displaced 32-bit x86 server market by ~2010

metric_value: 32-bit x86 server market was substantially displaced by x86-64 (EM64T/AMD64) by 2008-2010. By 2009, most new servers shipped with x86-64 processors. 32-bit-only server workloads became legacy by 2010.
notes: x86-64 did displace 32-bit; verify timeline | 32-bit x86 displacement confirmed. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Itanium ('By 2009, most servers were being shipped with x86-64 processors')

metric_value: Aberdeen Group (Peter Kastner, May 14 2004): Intel Itanium consistently outperforms 64-bit RISC-based servers; Intel-based platforms better performing per processor, more scalable, more cost-effective, and more flexible
notes: Cross-reference: Kastner Aberdeen Itanium report May 14 2004; cited in Intel white paper

metric_value: 94% of current Itanium-installed firms plan to buy more over next three years; 50% of non-buyers list it in future spend plans
notes: Forrester Research: Firms Stay The Course On Server Technologies; Brad Day and Frank Gillett July 14 2004

metric_name: BEA WebLogic case study: Itanium benchmark
metric_value: SpecjAppServer2002 leading benchmark results for absolute performance and price/performance all achieved using Intel architecture, majority on Itanium 2
notes: BEA JRockit JVM co-developed by Intel and BEA; optimized for IA-32 and Itanium

metric_value: Omicronn Trax on BEA WebLogic + Itanium 2: 5 million messages/hour at only 50-60% utilization; outperformed RISC alternatives
notes: Case study: financial transaction processing; Omicronn financial platform

metric_value: 64-bit processor transcends 4GB memory limit of 32-bit; can directly access virtually unlimited physical memory; dramatic performance for large memory-intensive applications
notes: Key 64-bit architectural advantage #1

metric_value: 64-bit processor can manipulate data in chunks twice as large (64-bit vs 32-bit); key for complex calculations requiring high precision
notes: Key 64-bit architectural advantage #2

metric_value: Itanium 2 processor supports up to 1 petabyte of physical memory; Xeon MP up to 1 terabyte; Xeon up to 64 gigabytes
notes: Memory scale differentiation between processor tiers

metric_name: RFID as driver of 64-bit data requirements
metric_value: RFID tags and point-of-sale devices causing quantum leap in processing and data requirements that may dwarf end-user workloads
notes: Giga Research cited (Forrester subsidiary); early RFID data volume prediction

metric_name: SOA and Web Services accelerating 64-bit need
metric_value: Rise of Web Services and Service Oriented Architecture (SOA) simplifying integration across businesses and supply chains; high-volume transactions in real-time; driving 64-bit demand
notes: Correctly forecasted SOA/web services driving compute scale requirements

metric_value: IDC: EM64T strategy provides excellent investment protection and additional headroom with few or no drawbacks; will initiate broad move toward 64-bit capable platforms
notes: IDC Al Gillen and Jean Bozman cited

metric_value: For some mid-tier enterprise applications, best platform choice not obvious; requires close look at software availability, business drivers, and workloads
notes: Nuanced positioning allowing both platforms to coexist

metric_value: EM64T/x86-64 will dominate general-purpose and mainstream enterprise applications; Itanium relegated to high-end business-critical niche
notes: Core prescient prediction; x86-64 did dominate; Itanium discontinued 2021

metric_value: Intel discontinued Itanium on July 29, 2021. Final orders accepted until January 30, 2020. This matched the predicted discontinuation.
notes: Intel discontinued Itanium in 2021; x86-64 dominated as predicted | Source notes confirm: 'Intel discontinued Itanium in 2021; x86-64 dominated as predicted.' Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Itanium
